channel.title: Heart Health & Training Tips
channel.description: Exercise science tips for heart health and performance

item.title: Weekly Cardio Recommendation
item.description: We recommend around 300 minutes each week of regular cardiovascular-focused exercise.

item.title: HR Zone 1
item.description: Aerobic, low Intensity (50-60% of your max heart rate)

item.title: HR Zone 2
item.description: Moderate, aerobic Intensity (60-70% of your max heart rate)

item.title: HR Zone 3
item.description: Moderate to High Aerobic Intensity (70-80% of max heart rate)

item.title: HR Zone 4
item.description: High, Anaerobic (80-90% of max heart rate)

item.title: HR Zone 5
item.description: Very High, Anerobic (90-100% of max heart rate)

item.title: Fuel Sources
item.description: There are three sources of fuel to move the body...carbs, fats and protein.

item.title: Anaerobic Movement
item.description: Movement that lasts from 10 seconds to 2 minutes is generally powered anaerobically (oxygen not required).

item.title: Aerobic Movement
item.description: After 2 minutes of exercise, the aerobic system will become the dominant system powering movement.

item.title: Fuel During Exercise
item.description: During exercise, we use all three sources of fuel. The dominate source depends on the time and effort of the exercise.

item.title: Muscle Fuel Storage
item.description: Some fuel is stored directly in the muscle fibre and is therefore readily available to power movement, quickly.

item.title: Weekly HIIT
item.description: For regular heart fitness, a good rule of thumb is to complete one session of true high intense interval training each week. (Zone 4 to 5)

item.title: Three Energy Systems
item.description: Phosphagen System for immediate energy. Anaerobic Glycolysis (30 seconds to a few minutes). Aerobic Metabolism using oxygen (longer than a few minutes)

item.title: Specificity Principle
item.description: The SPECIFICITY Principle applies to all training...the heart included. Slow exercise trains the aerobic system...intense exercise trains the anaerobic system.

item.title: Monitor Heart Rate
item.description: Monitoring your heart rate during exercise is a great way to understand which energy system you are using.

item.title: Conversation Test
item.description: How easy it is to carry on a conversation while you exercise helps you to understand how hard your heart is working.

item.title: Large Muscle Exercise
item.description: Large muscles of the body require more blood to power exercise...therefore a repeated squat movement can create great demand on your heart.

item.title: Small Muscle Exercise
item.description: Exercises using smaller muscles do not tax the heart as much as large muscles...therefore bicep curls will not place as large a demand on your heart as squats.

item.title: Max Heart Rate
item.description: The general rule of thumb to calculate your max heart rate is 220 – your age.

item.title: Runners: Lactate Threshold
item.description: Runners! Lactate threshold training is tempo training or race pace training. This is done close to your race date.

item.title: Runners: Interval Training
item.description: Runners! Interval Training is typically on a 1 to 1 work to rest ratio with each training time 3 to 5 minutes.

item.title: Runners: Aerobic Base
item.description: Runners! You should be able to have a conversation while targeting Aerobic Base Training or Long Slow Distance training.

item.title: HIIT Definition
item.description: HIIT stands for High Intense Interval Training.

item.title: HIIT Work-to-Rest
item.description: A great rest to work ratio for high intense interval training is one to one. i.e. one minute of high intense work followed by one minute of rest or light work.

item.title: Training Intensity
item.description: Are you truly training at a high intensity level? Monitor your heart rate to know for sure.

item.title: MAX VO2
item.description: MAX VO2 is the maximum amount of oxygen your body can use during intense exercise.

item.title: Norwegian Protocol
item.description: The Norwegian Protocol is a scientifically validated exercise work to rest ratio to improve your MAX VO2. Four minutes hard, 3 minutes lite, for four rounds.

item.title: Improve MAX VO2
item.description: Improving your MAX VO2 is a scientifically validated way to improve your heart health.
